label: verify-54950
system:
You are the citation verifier in a legal-exploration pipeline. You are given one authority as cited by the pipeline's components, plus snippets showing how it was used. Using web search (prefer primary/official sources: leginfo.legislature.ca.gov, courts.ca.gov, law.justia.com, casetext.com, codes.findlaw.com, fppc.ca.gov), determine:
1. Does the authority exist?
2. Is the citation form correct (reporter cite, year, section number)?
3. Does it plausibly stand for what the snippets use it for?

Statuses: VERIFIED (exists, cite essentially right, usage consistent with what you found); MISCHARACTERIZED (exists but does not support the claimed use, or the cite attaches to the wrong case/section); NOT_FOUND (cannot locate after genuine searching); CONTESTED (real sources point in conflicting directions, or scope is genuinely unsettled); UNCLEAR (could not determine within a reasonable search).

You are NOT determining whether the authority is still good law (no Shepardizing) — say what you actually confirmed, no more. Be strict: a close-but-wrong reporter cite or a holding stretched past what sources show is MISCHARACTERIZED, not VERIFIED.
prompt:
## Authority as cited (variants seen in the documents)

- Cal. Gov't Code § 54950
- Gov. Code § 54950
- Gov. Code §54950
- § 54950
- §54950

## How the documents used it

[delphi] …agged conflicting descriptions of (a) and whether the tech-communications provision sits in (b)(2) or (c)); Cal. Gov't Code § 54952.2(c) — possible location of technological/electronic-communications provision (verify); Cal. Gov't Code § 54950 — declaration of purpose favoring liberal construction toward openness; Cal. Gov't Code §§ 54960, 54960.1, 54960.5 — standing to sue, cure-and-correct mechanics, fee-shifting; Cal. Gov't Code § 54959 — misdemeanor liabi…

[tetlock] …ber intended contact or knew memory was shared. Intent may matter for discipline, remedy, or the broader 'developed a collective concurrence' analysis, but that's outside this sub-question.
- Liberal-construction canon (Gov. Code §54950's declared policy favoring public access, construed broadly against Brown Act evasion) will push a reviewing body/DA toward finding forfeiture rather than preserving the safe harbor in a novel AI fact pattern — courts h…

[dialectic] …ther member. (Verify precise statutory phrasing and subsection lettering; substance is well documented in Brown Act treatises and League of California Cities guidance, but I have not verified the exact clause text.)
- **Gov. Code § 54950** — legislative findings: the people "do not yield their sovereignty" and must remain informed to retain control over their public bodies — the interpretive backdrop for construing (b)(1) broadly.
- **Gov. Code §§ 54960…

## Context

The underlying question is a California public-agency hypothetical: q1 — The AI assistant as serial-meeting intermediary (Brown Act).

Verify this authority per your instructions.

canonical_cite.description: the correct full citation, if determinable

note.description: 1-3 sentences: what was confirmed or refuted, and the correct cite

text: {"status":"VERIFIED","canonical_cite":"Cal. Gov't Code § 54950 (Ralph M. Brown Act)","note":"Confirmed via leginfo.legislature.ca.gov: § 54950 is the Brown Act's legislative findings/declaration of purpose, stating that public agencies exist to conduct the people's business openly, that \"the people ... do not yield their sovereignty\" to their agencies, and that they must remain informed to retain control over their government. This matches its use in all three documents as the liberal-construction/interpretive-backdrop provision favoring openness and broad construction against evasion — a well-established, textbook use of this section.","source_urls":["https://leginfo.legislature.ca.gov/faces/codes_displaySection.xhtml?lawCode=GOV&sectionNum=54950","https://codes.findlaw.com/ca/government-code/gov-sect-54950.html","https://law.justia.com/codes/california/2021/code-gov/title-5/division-2/part-1/chapter-9/section-54950/"]}

data.canonical_cite: Cal. Gov't Code § 54950 (Ralph M. Brown Act)
data.note: Confirmed via leginfo.legislature.ca.gov: § 54950 is the Brown Act's legislative findings/declaration of purpose, stating that public agencies exist to conduct the people's business openly, that "the people ... do not yield their sovereignty" to their agencies, and that they must remain informed to retain control over their government. This matches its use in all three documents as the liberal-construction/interpretive-backdrop provision favoring openness and broad construction against evasion — a well-established, textbook use of this section.
